title: Contributing to shinytone
text: Small fixes (typos, doc clarifications, obvious bugs) welcome direct pull requests. larger changes, please open issue first discuss approach investing significant time. development workflow standard R-package one: Fork repo clone fork. Create feature branch main. Make changes. Run devtools::document() (regenerate man/) devtools::test() (run unit tests). Run devtools::check() aim 0 errors / 0 warnings. (One persistent NOTE “unused Imports” expected ignorable; relates bundled Shiny app.) Open pull request main clear description changed .

title: Contributing to shinytone

Keep new exported functions consistent existing API: long-format data , named column arguments, defaults match bundled sample_f0 schema reasonable.

title: Getting started with shinytone
text: examples assume three packages loaded:
code: library(shinytone) library(dplyr) library(ggplot2)

title: Getting started with shinytone
text: shinytone ships real citation-tone corpus available immediately library(shinytone). contains 38,808 f0 samples 1,848 tokens across 13 speakers. columns ’ll use: token — unique identifier recorded syllable (groups rows contour together) time — time seconds within token f0_Hz — fundamental frequency speaker — speaker ID tone — Chao tone category (1-5) char — Chinese character syllable See ?sample_f0 full schema source citation.

title: Getting started with shinytone
text: Raw Hz isn’t comparable across speakers (men women different baseline f0). normalise_f0() adds speaker_mean column plus either f0_st (semitones, default) f0_zscore, computed per speaker: Now f0_st puts every speaker comparable scale. plot mean contour per tone, use [compute_mean_contour()], normalises time [0, 1] within token averaging. package function returns result time / f0_predicted / tone schema used [predict_gca()] [predict_gamm()]:
code: normed <- normalise_f0(sample_f0,                        f0          = "f0_Hz",                        speaker     = "speaker",                        tone        = "tone",                        method      = "semitone",                        mean_method = "weighted") head(normed[, c("speaker", "tone", "f0_Hz", "speaker_mean", "f0_st")]) #>   speaker tone    f0_Hz speaker_mean     f0_st #> 1   dc102    3 245.7078     237.0675 0.6197450 #> 2   dc102    3 246.4516     237.0675 0.6720735 #> 3   dc102    3 247.5111     237.0675 0.7463436 #> 4   dc102    3 248.5496     237.0675 0.8188289 #> 5   dc102    3 249.7015     237.0675 0.8988754 #> 6   dc102    3 251.6647     237.0675 1.0344568 mean_contour <- compute_mean_contour(normed,                                      token = "token", f0 = "f0_st",                                      time  = "time",  tone = "tone")  ggplot(mean_contour, aes(time, f0_predicted, colour = factor(tone))) +   geom_line(linewidth = 1) +   labs(x = "Normalised time", y = "f0 (semitones)", colour = "Tone")

what: Inspect for outliers and pitch-tracking artefacts
title: Getting started with shinytone
text: inspect_f0() runs three complementary checks: tokens whose per-token max min sits z_threshold SDs speaker’s mean (extreme-value), tokens whose median unusual speaker tone (token-level), individual samples rate f0 change exceeds physiological plausibility (sample-level; Sundberg 1973, Steffman & Cole 2022). flag_notes column gives human-readable reason flagged sample (e.g., "max high; jump (rise)"). live app, Inspect tab lets click flagged tokens one one.
code: inspected <- inspect_f0(sample_f0,                         f0      = "f0_Hz",                         token   = "token",                         time    = "time",                         speaker = "speaker",                         tone    = "tone")  # How many tokens were flagged overall? inspected |>   distinct(token, .keep_all = TRUE) |>   count(flagged_token) #> # A tibble: 2 × 2 #>   flagged_token     n #>   <lgl>         <int> #> 1 FALSE          1586 #> 2 TRUE            262

title: Getting started with shinytone
text: fit_polynomial() returns one row per token Legendre polynomial coefficients (c0, c1, c2, …). Useful features downstream classifiers, compact summary contour shape. Interpretation: c0 ≈ token-level mean f0 (semitones, relative speaker mean) c1 ≈ linear slope across contour (positive = rising) c2 ≈ curvature (positive = U-shaped, negative = inverted-U)
code: poly_coefs <- fit_polynomial(normed,                              f0      = "f0_st",                              token   = "token",                              time    = "time",                              speaker = "speaker",                              tone    = "tone",                              degree  = 2) head(poly_coefs) #> # A tibble: 6 × 6 #>   token            speaker  tone    c0    c1    c2 #>   <chr>            <chr>   <int> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> #> 1 dc102一1s22.3525 dc102       6 -1.53  3.86 0.114 #> 2 dc102一2s22.9425 dc102       6 -1.07  2.62 0.282 #> 3 dc102一3s23.5725 dc102       6 -1.37  2.79 0.474 #> 4 dc102一4s24.1425 dc102       6 -1.14  1.76 0.732 #> 5 dc102一5s24.7925 dc102       6 -1.09  1.90 0.296 #> 6 dc102一6s25.3125 dc102       6 -1.87  2.14 1.28

title: Getting started with shinytone
text: fit_gca() convenience wrapper around lme4::lmer() sensible defaults tone-shape modelling — orthogonal polynomials per-token normalised time, plus conventional random effects speaker item. non-standard model structures, call lme4::lmer() directly.
code: # (Skipped on package build for speed; uncomment to run locally.) gca <- fit_gca(normed,                f0      = "f0_st",                time    = "time",                token   = "token",                tone    = "tone",                speaker = "speaker",                item    = "char",                 # use Chinese char as item                degree  = 2,                random_slope_speaker = FALSE,                random_slope_item    = FALSE)  # Population-level per-tone curves preds <- predict_gca(gca, n = 100) ggplot(preds, aes(time, f0_predicted, colour = tone)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 1)

title: Getting started with shinytone
text: contour_to_chao() reduces per-tone mean (model-predicted) contours Chao numerals — 5-level digit strings like 55, 35, 214. Three conversion methods one pass: refline — reference-line , rounded [1, 5] scale interval — interval-based , ceiling (0, 5] scale robust — robust using μ ± σ highest- lowest-tone extremes (requires raw_data) shape column summarises contour (“rising”, “falling”, “dipping”, etc.) derived reference-line numeral via classify_contour().
code: # Pre-aggregate into a per-tone mean contour over all speakers mean_contour <- compute_mean_contour(sample_f0,                                      token = "token", f0 = "f0_Hz",                                      time  = "time",  tone = "tone")  chao <- contour_to_chao(   mean_contour,   raw_data  = sample_f0,   raw_token = "token", raw_f0 = "f0_Hz", raw_tone = "tone" ) chao[, c("tone", "refline", "interval", "robust", "shape")] #>   tone refline interval robust         shape #> 1    1      22       22     33 mid-low level #> 2    2     212      111    222       dipping #> 3    3      32       32     33       falling #> 4    4      45       45     44        rising #> 5    5      22       21     32 mid-low level #> 6    6      23       13     23        rising

title: shinytone — A Citation Tone Research Hub
text: shinytone integrates full citation-tone analysis workflow single place: pitch extraction, -speaker f0 normalisation, growth-curve generalised additive mixed-effects models, outlier artefact inspection, Chao tone numeral summarisation. designed phoneticians, typologists, fieldworkers, students working lexical tone production.

title: shinytone — A Citation Tone Research Hub
text: 🎙️ F0 processing: extract f0 .wav files wrassp Praat, including downloadable Praat script tailored settings. 🔍 Inspect: token-level outlier flags (-speaker z-score) sample-level artefact detection (octave jumps, rate--change violations, carryover frames; Steffman & Cole 2022). 📏 Normalise: -speaker semitone z-score normalisation, simple weighted speaker means. 📈 Visualise: f0 contours coloured tone, optional faceting speaker. 📊 Model: per-token Legendre polynomials, growth-curve analysis (GCA) orthogonal polynomials, generalised additive mixed models (GAMM) optional AR1 correction. 🧮 Summarise: convert contours Chao tone numerals using three methods (reference-line, interval, robust).

what: Apply tone re-labels and exclusions to a long-format f0 table — apply_relabels
title: Apply tone re-labels and exclusions to a long-format f0 table — apply_relabels
text: Adds annotation-curation columns long-format f0 data frame without touching original tone labels. Tokens named relabel receive new value tone_relabelled; tokens named exclude marked TRUE excluded. tokens keep original tone tone_relabelled FALSE excluded. Curation operates token level: every row belonging token receives token's relabel / exclusion.

title: Apply tone re-labels and exclusions to a long-format f0 table — apply_relabels
code: apply_relabels(   data,   token = "token",   tone = "tone",   relabel = NULL,   exclude = character(0),   note = NULL )

title: Apply tone re-labels and exclusions to a long-format f0 table — apply_relabels
text: data long-format data frame one row per f0 sample. token Column name token ID. Default "token". tone Column name (original) tone category. Default "tone". relabel named character vector mapping token IDs (names) new tone label (values); NULL relabelling. Tokens named keep original tone. exclude character vector token IDs mark excluded; empty vector none. note named character vector mapping token IDs (names) free-text reason curation decision (e.g. "literary form", "sandhi form"); NULL none. Tokens named get NA.

title: Apply tone re-labels and exclusions to a long-format f0 table — apply_relabels
text: data three appended columns: tone_relabelled: character, curated tone label (original tone relabel applies). excluded: logical, TRUE tokens exclude. curate_note: character, reason recorded token (NA).

title: Apply tone re-labels and exclusions to a long-format f0 table — apply_relabels
text: original tone column never modified, curation decision always reversible auditable. Downstream analyses can either group tone_relabelled (treating variant category, e.g. T4-colloquial vs T4-literary) /drop rows excluded TRUE. annotation step, signal-repair step: assumes f0 values already clean (see inspect_f0() F0 Correction tab repairing mis-tracked f0). Variant patterns often discovered visually surfaced token-level register check flag_level_outliers(), flags tokens whose overall level unusual speaker tone — natural seed relabelling.

title: Apply tone re-labels and exclusions to a long-format f0 table — apply_relabels
code: data(sample_f0) # Relabel two tokens to a literary-reading variant, exclude one mis-elicited out <- apply_relabels(sample_f0,                       token   = "token",                       tone    = "tone",                       relabel = c("t4_03" = "T4lit", "t4_07" = "T4lit"),                       exclude = c("t5_11")) table(out$tone, out$tone_relabelled, useNA = "ifany") #>     #>        1    2    3    4    5    6 #>   1 6510    0    0    0    0    0 #>   2    0 6531    0    0    0    0 #>   3    0    0 6426    0    0    0 #>   4    0    0    0 6468    0    0 #>   5    0    0    0    0 6384    0 #>   6    0    0    0    0    0 6489

title: Diagnostics for choosing the number of clusters (candidate tones) — choose_k_f0

title: Diagnostics for choosing the number of clusters (candidate tones) — choose_k_f0
text: feat list cluster_features(), numeric feature matrix. k_range Integer vector cluster counts evaluate. Default 2:8. nstart k-means restarts. Default 25. gap Whether compute gap statistic (needs cluster package). Default TRUE. bending MDL bending factor (>= 1; Kaland & Ellison 2023). Higher values -weight model/residual cost fewer clusters preferred; tune MDL (information-cost) curve U-shaped. Default 1.

what: Classify a Chao tone numeral string as a shape — classify_contour
title: Classify a Chao tone numeral string as a shape — classify_contour
text: Returns short human-readable shape name (e.g., "level", "rising", "falling", "dipping", "peaking", one finer-grained "low level" / "mid level" / "high level" labels repeated-digit strings). Used contour_to_chao() add shape column output, also exposed standalone utility classifying numerals external sources.

title: Classify a Chao tone numeral string as a shape — classify_contour
code: classify_contour(chao_str)

title: Classify a Chao tone numeral string as a shape — classify_contour
text: chao_str character string Chao digits, e.g. "55", "214", "35".

title: Classify a Chao tone numeral string as a shape — classify_contour
text: character string naming shape.

title: Classify a Chao tone numeral string as a shape — classify_contour
text: Classification rules: digits identical: one "low level", "mid-low level", "mid level", "mid-high level", "high level" according digit value. Three digits interior minimum endpoints: "dipping". Three digits interior maximum endpoints: "peaking". Otherwise, "rising" last digit higher first, "falling" lower, "level" equal.

title: Classify a Chao tone numeral string as a shape — classify_contour
text: Chao, Y. R. (1930). system tone-letters. Le Maître Phonétique, 30, 24–27. Xu, C., & Zhang, C. (2024). cross-linguistic review citation tone production studies: Methodology recommendations. Journal Acoustical Society America, 156(4), 2538–2565. doi:10.1121/10.0032356

title: Classify a Chao tone numeral string as a shape — classify_contour
code: classify_contour("55")    # "high level" #> [1] "high level" classify_contour("214")   # "dipping" #> [1] "dipping" classify_contour("35")    # "rising" #> [1] "rising" classify_contour("51")    # "falling" #> [1] "falling"

what: Build per-token feature vectors for f0-contour clustering — cluster_features
title: Build per-token feature vectors for f0-contour clustering — cluster_features
text: Resamples every token's f0 contour common length turns fixed-length feature vector suitable clustering. f0 already speaker-normalised (e.g. f0_st normalise_f0()) clusters reflect tone shape register rather high voice.

title: Build per-token feature vectors for f0-contour clustering — cluster_features
code: cluster_features(   data,   f0 = "f0",   token = "token",   time = "time",   speaker = NULL,   tone = NULL,   n_points = 20L,   features = c("points", "legendre", "dct", "derivative"),   degree = 4L,   register = c("level", "shape") )

title: Build per-token feature vectors for f0-contour clustering — cluster_features
text: data Long-format data frame, one row per f0 sample. f0, token, time Column names. time normalised per token. speaker Optional speaker column, carried metadata. tone Optional tone column, carried later validation. n_points Number points resample contour . Default 20. features Feature representation: "points" (resampled contour), "legendre" (orthogonal-polynomial coefficients degree), "dct" (low-order discrete-cosine coefficients), "derivative" (first difference: cluster rate change / movement, discarding height). degree Order "legendre"/"dct" features. Default 4. register "level" keeps contour's height (high vs low level tones separate); "shape" centres contour mean 0 (cluster shape ).

title: Build per-token feature vectors for f0-contour clustering — cluster_features
text: list features (token x p matrix used clustering), contours (token x n_points resampled f0, plotting cluster means), tokens, meta (token / speaker / tone).

text: data frame one row per tone, containing: tone: tone label. n_digits: number digits numeral (2 3). refline: reference-line method numeral, e.g. "55". interval: interval-based numeral, e.g. "45". robust: robust numeral (NA raw_data given). shape: shape name classify_contour() applied refline. sample_times, ref_continuous, int_continuous, rob_continuous: list columns per-sample numeric values underlying digit strings, useful plotting analysis. Two attributes attached returned data frame: f0_min f0_max input contour, plus robust_stats list containing means standard deviations used robust method (NULL raw_data supplied).

title: Convert per-tone contours to Chao tone numerals — contour_to_chao
text: three use Fraction Range () normalisation, maps f0 onto bounded scale representing speaker's (corpus's) pitch range. differ range defined scaled values mapped Chao digits. Reference-line [1, 5]: f0' = (f0 - f0_min) / (f0_max - f0_min) * 4 + 1, rounded. Five reference lines integer values; sampled point snaps nearest integer. Interval-based (0, 5]: f0' = (f0 - f0_min) / (f0_max - f0_min) * 5, ceilinged. Five equal-width intervals; sampled point assigned interval via ceiling(). Robust (0, 5] (raw_data supplied): interval formula, range defined μ ± σ per-token f0 peaks highest-pitched tone (max) per-token f0 valleys lowest-pitched tone (min), following Zhu (1999) recommendation Xu & Zhang (2024). robust outliers using corpus-wide min max.

title: Convert per-tone contours to Chao tone numerals — contour_to_chao
text: tone, function samples contour either two endpoints (yielding 2-digit numeral) two endpoints plus one interior turning point (yielding 3-digit numeral). 3-digit case applies either: interior minimum threshold Chao units endpoints (dip), interior maximum threshold Chao units endpoints (peak). default threshold = 0.5 (half Chao unit [1, 5] scale) conservative choice. Lower thresholds produce 3-digit numerals, useful tonally rich languages.

title: Normalise f0 by speaker — normalise_f0
text: Adds within-speaker normalised f0 column long-format f0 data frame. Speaker normalisation puts contours male female speakers comparable scale, near-universal pre-processing step cross-speaker tone modelling, plotting, contour summarisation (Rose 1987; Zhu 1999). Two formulae available. Semitones speaker's mean give perceptually uniform scale. -speaker z-scores combine centring unit-variance scaling. formulae rely per-speaker mean f0, can computed two ways: simple arithmetic mean observations, mean per-tone means every tone contributes equally speaker's centre.

Default "f0". speaker Name speaker ID column. Default "speaker". tone Name tone category column. Default "tone". read mean_method = "weighted". method Normalisation formula. One : "semitone" (default): ST = 12 * log2(f0 / speaker_mean). Result 0 speaker mean ±12 one octave . "zscore": Z = (f0 - speaker_mean) / sd(speaker_f0). Result mean approximately 0 SD approximately 1 within speaker. mean_method per-speaker mean used method computed: "weighted" (default; mean per-tone means) "simple" (arithmetic mean across observations).

text: New flag_level_outliers(): third inspection layer compares token’s overall level (median f0, semitones) tokens speaker tone using robust modified z-score (median/MAD; Iglewicz & Hoaglin 1993, cutoff 3.5). flags smoothly shifted contours — e.g. low-tone token mis-tracked mid-tone band — pooled max/min check sample-level jump check miss. Surfaced inspect_f0() (new level_threshold min_tokens arguments, level high / level low notes) Inspect tab. "norm" (normalised-time) option removed time_unit flag_pitch_jumps(), inspect_f0(), Inspect tab: rate-- change thresholds physiological (ST per 10 ms) meaning real time discarded. Inspection runs real-time data (s / ms). Inspect-tab guide rewritten around three complementary layers, “Pitch-tracking quality check” workflow now marks Normalise optional (inspection runs raw f0).

title: shinytone (development version)
text: flag_pitch_jumps() (therefore inspect_f0() Inspect tab): sample--sample jump detected, flag now placed whichever side jump farther token’s median f0, rather always landing sample. correctly identifies artefact whether sits start end sequence (e.g. octave doubling first frame token, previous landing-logic mis-flagged). Carryover now walks forward backward flagged sample, artefact runs begin end token extended directions. Inspect-tab guide text flag_pitch_jumps()’s function docs describe new median-aware logic adaptation rate--change + carryover approach Steffman & Cole (2022).

title: shinytone (development version)
text: First public release R package, alongside existing online Shiny app https://chenzixu.shinyapps.io/shinytone/. Package skin stage: Shiny app continues run unchanged. Standalone analytical functions (normalise_f0(), fit_gca(), fit_gamm(), contour_to_chao(), …) extracted subsequent releases.
